name: Switzerland
summary: World-leading software pay, acute ICT shortage and low income tax make Switzerland a high-earning destination for this couple, but it is a poor settle-and-naturalise route: 10-year citizenship, a tiny 8,500 non-EU quota, only a 6-month non-renewable post-study permit, and no reliable family reunion for a master's student so the spouse cannot accompany.

links.title: State Secretariat for Migration (SEM, official)
Embassy of Switzerland in Bangladesh (EDA, official)

job-market.summary: Record ICT shortage (72% of firms can't fill dev roles) and ~CHF 128k median pay; both absorbable but gated by 8,500 non-EU quota.
job-market.reasoning: Switzerland faces an all-time-high skills shortage: digitalswitzerland's 2024 Digital Skills Barometer found 72% of employers struggle to fill software/IT roles, with a projected shortfall of ~54,400 ICT specialists by 2033. Zurich software pay is world-leading (median ~CHF 123,000-128,000; seniors CHF 167k+), and demand for cloud/data/AI stays intense in Zurich, Geneva and Lausanne, easily large enough for both spouses. The brake is the non-EU quota: only 8,500 third-country permits for 2026 (4,500 B + 4,000 L), with employers required to prove no EU/EFTA candidate was available, making placement of two non-EU engineers materially harder than in quota-free Germany.
evidence: digitalswitzerland 2024: 72% of employers can't fill software/IT roles (record high)
Projected shortfall ~54,400 ICT specialists by 2033
Zurich SW engineer median ~CHF 123,000-128,000 (Glassdoor/SalaryExpert 2025-26); seniors CHF 167k+
2026 non-EU quota frozen at 8,500 (4,500 B + 4,000 L permits)
Employer must prove no EU/EFTA candidate before hiring a non-EU worker

links.title: Swiss immigration quotas for 2026 (Fragomen)
Software Engineer salary in Zurich 2026 (SalaryExpert/ERI)

visa-access.summary: National visas decided in Dhaka (Gulshan 2, no New Delhi travel), student visa free; but ~8-12wk cantonal-approval chain plus quota gating.
visa-access.reasoning: The Embassy of Switzerland in Dhaka (Bay's Edgewater, Gulshan 2) handles and decides national (type D) student and work visas locally, with no New Delhi routing; appointments are by phone/email and the student visa is free. The slow part is the chain: a Swiss canton must authorise first, so the realistic timeline is ~8-12+ weeks, Bengali documents need certified DE/FR/IT/EN translation, and work visas additionally depend on the non-EU quota and labour-market test. Local decisioning and a free student route keep this in the good band, but cantonal lead time and quota uncertainty stop it climbing higher.
evidence: Embassy Dhaka (Gulshan 2) handles/decides national visas; no New Delhi travel
Appointments by phone (+880 2 5505 1601) or email (dhaka.visa@eda.admin.ch)
Student visa application window opens 6 months out; apply >=3 months ahead
Total process ~8-12+ weeks (canton must authorise before embassy issues)
Work visa routed via VFS Global Dhaka once Swiss work authorisation granted

links.title: Visa - Entry to and residence in Switzerland (EDA Bangladesh, official)
Embassy of Switzerland in Bangladesh (EDA, official)

citizenship.summary: 10 years lawful residence + C permit required; dual citizenship allowed (no renunciation); language bar B1 spoken / A2 written.
citizenship.reasoning: Ordinary naturalisation requires 10 years of federal residence (3 of the last 5 before applying) AND holding a C settlement permit at the time, far longer than this couple's faster-citizenship preference. The clear positive is that Switzerland permits dual nationality and does not require renouncing Bangladeshi citizenship. Applicants must prove integration plus language at the federal minimum (B1 spoken / A2 written in a national language), with cantonal/communal processing on top of the 10 years. Dual-allowed and a strong passport lift it off the floor, but the decade-plus timeline and language requirement cap it in the mixed band.
evidence: Ordinary naturalisation: 10 years federal residence (3 of last 5) + C permit at application
Dual citizenship allowed; no renunciation of Bangladeshi nationality required by Swiss law
Language: B1 spoken / A2 written in a national language (federal minimum)
Cantonal/communal residence rules and an integration assessment apply on top
Study (L) years do not count; B and C permit years count

links.title: State Secretariat for Migration - becoming Swiss (SEM, official)
Swiss citizenship by naturalisation (Richmond Chambers, 2026 guide)

post-study-work.summary: Only a 6-month, non-renewable job-search L permit (max 15h/week); convert to B if hired, but L pool often exhausted by October and no spouse track.
post-study-work.reasoning: Non-EU graduates of accredited Swiss universities get only a 6-month, non-renewable short-stay (L) job-search permit; you may work at most 15 hours/week during the search and must apply at least 15 days before finishing studies. Converting to a B work permit requires a job offer plus the employer proving no Swiss/EU candidate could fill it, and the non-EU L pool has been exhausted by mid-October in recent years, so timing risk is real. The window is far shorter than Germany's 18 months or NZ/Canada's multi-year open work, and there is no parallel spouse-of-graduate work route - weak for a couple who must both work.
evidence: Post-study job-search permit: 6 months, not renewable (L permit)
Max 15h/week work during the search; apply >=15 days before graduating
Conversion to B requires job offer + employer labour-market test (no Swiss/EU candidate)
Non-EU L quota pool exhausted by mid-October in recent years (timing risk)
No dedicated spouse-of-graduate work permit during the search phase

links.title: Permit for post-studies job search, non-EU/EFTA (Canton Geneva, official)
Post-graduation stay in Switzerland (EPFL, official)

spouse-family.summary: Decisive weakness: non-EU students have no family-reunion entitlement, so the spouse can't reliably accompany a master's student; only B/C spouses work.
spouse-family.reasoning: For a couple that must relocate together and both work, the study phase is a near-disqualifier: non-EU/EFTA students have no legal entitlement to family reunification, and cantons rarely approve a spouse to join an ordinary master's student (discretionary Art. 44 FNIA - 'may be granted', conditional on housing and self-sufficiency). The work phase is fine: a spouse joining a B or C permit holder may work or self-employ anywhere in Switzerland. The realistic fix is for both to enrol as students or for one to land a B work permit first, but the inability of a student's spouse to accompany and work makes the study-led pathway weak for this profile.
evidence: Non-EU students: no entitlement to family reunification (study phase)
Spouse joining a non-EU sponsor is discretionary under Art. 44 FNIA ('may be granted')
Art. 44 conditions: cohabitation, suitable housing, financial self-sufficiency
Spouse of a B/C permit holder may work or self-employ Switzerland-wide
Workaround: both enrol separately, or one secures a B work permit first

links.title: Swiss spouse & family reunification, non-EU (Richmond Chambers, 2026 guide)
Family reunification (binational.ch, NGO guide)

msc-study.summary: Elite English-taught CS MSc at ETH/EPFL but tuition tripled to >=CHF 2,190/sem from autumn 2025; no work first 6 months, then 15h/week.
msc-study.reasoning: ETH Zurich and EPFL offer world-class fully-English MSc Computer Science programmes (120 ECTS), and Swiss public tuition is still cheap globally - but from autumn 2025 fees for incoming international students tripled from CHF 730 to at least CHF 2,190 per semester (~CHF 4,380/year, indexed to CPI from 2029). Non-EU students cannot work for the first 6 months and are then capped at 15h/week in term (full-time in breaks). The decisive limiter for this couple is study-phase family reunion (see spouse-family): the spouse cannot reliably accompany a master's student, so abundant programmes and modest fees only partly offset that.
evidence: ETH Zurich and EPFL MSc Computer Science fully taught in English (120 ECTS)
International tuition tripled from autumn 2025: CHF 730 -> >=CHF 2,190/semester
Non-EU students: no work in first 6 months, then max 15h/week in term
Full-time work allowed in lecture-free periods
Spouse cannot reliably accompany a non-EU master's student (study-phase reunion)

links.title: Increase in tuition fees at ETH Zurich and EPFL (EPFL, official)
Employment for non-EU/EFTA students (ETH Zurich, official)

pr-pathway.summary: C permit (PR) normally after 10 years incl. 5 on a B permit; discretionary fast-track at 5 years needs high integration + B1 language.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Standard settlement (C permit) comes after 10 years of lawful residence, including 5 uninterrupted years on a B permit. A discretionary 'C permit anticipe' is possible after 5 years for third-country nationals showing a high degree of integration (B1 language, economic self-sufficiency, clean record), which two well-paid engineers can plausibly meet. It is clearer than long quota-locked routes, but ~5 years best-case (10 standard), a discretionary case-by-case assessment, and the fact that study (L) years do not count put it below points-based PR systems granting residence in 2-3 years.
evidence: Standard C permit after 10 years residence incl. 5 uninterrupted on a B permit
Discretionary fast-track C permit at 5 years with high integration (B1 + self-sufficiency)
Third-country nationals must apply proactively; decision case-by-case, not a points test
Study (L) job-search years do not count toward the residence requirement
Realistic ~5yr best case for two high earners; 10yr standard

links.title: Swiss C Permit (permanent residence), 2026 guide (Richmond Chambers)
Living and working in Switzerland (SEM, official)

income-cost.summary: World-leading net SW pay (~CHF 89.6k on CHF 120k) but Zurich city 1-beds now CHF 1,700-2,600/mo plus CHF 393/mo health premiums bite.
income-cost.reasoning: Software engineers earn among the highest net salaries globally: a Zurich resident on ~CHF 120,000 keeps ~CHF 89,572 (25.4% average tax) before social charges, and two incomes give strong savings potential. Offsets have grown: a Zurich city-centre 1-bed now runs ~CHF 1,700-2,600/month (up from prior ~CHF 1,480-1,650), mandatory health insurance averages CHF 393.30/month per adult in 2026 (paid separately), and prices are among the world's highest. Two engineering incomes still bank meaningful surplus, but rising rents trim this a point below prior.
evidence: Zurich net: CHF 120,000 -> ~CHF 89,572 (25.4% avg tax), before social charges
Zurich city-centre 1-bed now ~CHF 1,700-2,600/month (2025-26 data, up from prior)
2026 average health premium CHF 393.30/month per adult (separate cost)
General price level among the world's highest (housing the biggest expense)
Two SW incomes -> strong real savings despite high costs

links.title: Switzerland - taxes on personal income (PwC Tax Summaries)
Cost of living in Zurich 2026 (Wise)

healthcare.summary: Top-quality universal care via mandatory individual insurance: avg CHF 393.30/mo per adult in 2026 (+4.4%), plus deductibles.
healthcare.reasoning: Switzerland delivers world-class healthcare with universal coverage, but it runs on compulsory private basic insurance every resident must buy individually - averaging CHF 393.30/month per adult in 2026 (canton spread is wide; Zug premiums actually fell 14.7%), rising 4.4% on average, on top of annual deductibles and a 10% co-pay. Quality and access are excellent with no waiting-list culture, but newcomers carry a real monthly cost from day one with no employer-paid statutory pool, so it lands below tax-funded universal systems.
evidence: Universal coverage via mandatory individual basic insurance
2026 average premium CHF 393.30/month per adult (+4.4%; +CHF 16.60)
Wide canton spread; Zug premiums fell 14.7% while Ticino rose 7.1%
Annual deductible (franchise) + 10% co-pay on top of premiums
Top-tier quality and access; no employer statutory pool for newcomers

links.title: Premiums and costs - FAQs (Federal Office of Public Health BAG, official)
Swiss health premiums to rise by 4.4% on average (swissinfo, 2026)

culture-language.summary: English works in tech and livability is world-class, but a national language (B1) is needed for officialdom, the fast-track C permit and citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: International tech workplaces in Zurich, Geneva and Lausanne operate heavily in English and livability/safety are world-class, but Switzerland is officially German/French/Italian and a national language is required for daily bureaucracy, the fast-track C permit and naturalisation (~B1 spoken). The couple's settle-and-naturalise goal therefore runs into a genuine language bar, and SVP rhetoric explicitly targets 'expatriates who speak only English'. Strong day-to-day English usability lifts it, but the officialdom language requirement holds it in the mixed band.
evidence: English common in Zurich/Geneva/Lausanne tech; high livability and safety
National language (~B1) needed for naturalisation and the fast-track C permit
Four official languages; officialdom runs in the cantonal language
Foreign residents ~26% of population (high diversity)
SVP rhetoric targets English-only 'expatriates' - real integration friction

links.title: Living and integration in Switzerland (SEM, official)
Swiss citizenship by naturalisation - language (Richmond Chambers)

safety-law.summary: WJP Rule of Law 2025 ~0.81 (just outside top-10 of 143; Japan 15th), very low crime, strong independent courts; migrant treatment fair.
safety-law.reasoning: Switzerland sits in the global top tier of the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (143 countries) with a score around 0.81, just below the top-10 cluster (Denmark 0.90 leads; Germany/Luxembourg/New Zealand ~0.83; Japan ranks 15th), reflecting very low violent crime, strong order and security, and reliable independent courts. Treatment of skilled migrants is generally fair. Minor temper: direct-democracy votes targeting minorities (2009 minaret ban, 2021 face-covering ban) show an exclusionary streak, but personal-safety and rule-of-law fundamentals remain excellent for this couple.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 143 countries; Switzerland in global top tier (~0.81, just outside top-10)
Top-10 cluster scores ~0.82-0.90 (Denmark 0.90); Japan ranked 15th for reference
Very low violent crime; strong order and security
Independent, well-functioning courts; fair legal process
Constitutional minority votes (minaret 2009, face-covering 2021) a caveat

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 global press release (World Justice Project)
WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 - Switzerland country page

politics.summary: Very stable institutions; SVP 10-million population cap rejected 14.06.2026 (~55% against), but SVP largest party and non-EU quotas stay frozen.
politics.reasoning: Switzerland's consensus government and direct democracy make policy unusually stable and predictable. On 14 June 2026 voters rejected the SVP's 'No to 10 million Switzerland' population-cap initiative (~55% against), a clear pro-openness signal that lifts this above prior, but the SVP remains the largest party and immigration is a permanent flashpoint, with strict annual non-EU quotas frozen at 8,500 for 2026. Skilled-migration routes are intact and stable, yet persistent anti-immigration mobilisation and quota gating keep this short of the top band.
evidence: Stable consensus federal government; predictable, slow-moving policy
SVP 10-million population-cap initiative rejected 14.06.2026 (~55% against)
SVP largest party; immigration a recurring referendum theme
2026 non-EU quotas frozen at 8,500 (predictable but capped)
Skilled-worker / B-permit routes preserved

links.title: Swiss reject right-wing bid to cap population at 10 million (NPR, 15.06.2026)
Federal Council leaves third-country quotas for 2026 unchanged (admin.ch, official)

tax.summary: Low income tax + low social charges; Zurich CHF 120k nets ~CHF 89.6k (25.4% avg); married 'splitting' relief; B-permit pay taxed at source.
tax.reasoning: Switzerland is genuinely low-tax for high earners: a Zurich resident on CHF 120,000 pays ~CHF 30,428 income tax (25.4% average, 37% marginal) and keeps ~CHF 89,572, with employee social contributions low (~6-7%) versus Germany's ~20%. Married couples benefit from income 'splitting', and Zurich cut taxation 5.3-11.4% from 1 January 2025. B-permit holders are taxed at source (withholding) with mandatory ordinary assessment above CHF 120,000 gross. The main caveat is that mandatory health premiums sit outside payroll, but on income-tax burden and take-home this is a clear strength for two SW engineers.
evidence: Zurich CHF 120,000: ~CHF 30,428 tax (25.4% avg, 37% marginal), net ~CHF 89,572
Employee social contributions low (~6-7%) vs ~20% Germany
Married couples taxed via income 'splitting' (favourable for dual earners)
Zurich reduced taxation 5.3-11.4% from 01.01.2025 (Nov-2024 referendum)
B-permit pay taxed at source; ordinary assessment mandatory above CHF 120,000 gross

links.title: Switzerland - taxes on personal income (PwC Tax Summaries)
Withholding (source) tax for foreign employees (ETH Zurich, official)

muslim-diaspora.summary: ~470-490k Muslims (5.4%) and easy halal in cities, but a tiny Bangladeshi community (~3,000) and constitutional minaret/face-covering bans.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Switzerland has roughly 470,000-490,000 Muslims (~5.4% of residents, mostly Turkish/Albanian/Bosnian-origin) with ~260 mosques/prayer rooms and easy halal food in Zurich, Geneva and Bern. But the Bangladeshi/South-Asian community is very small (~3,000 Bangladeshis), so culturally specific support is thin, and sentiment carries a hard edge: the 2009 minaret-construction ban and 2021 face-covering ban are in the constitution, and SVP campaigns invoke 'creeping Islamization'. Good halal access offsets, but the small community plus institutionalised hostility hold this in the weak band.
evidence: ~470,000-490,000 Muslims (~5.4% of residents); ~260 mosques/prayer rooms
Halal food easy to find in Zurich, Geneva, Bern
Very small Bangladeshi community (~3,000)
Constitutional minaret ban (2009) and face-covering ban (2021)
SVP 'Islamization' rhetoric; periodic anti-Muslim incidents
